Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Apr-04-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Judy H. Jung, Esq., DeCarlo & Shanley, 213-488-4100, appearing for Defendnats UBC, Clafferty, UCB Job; (Court Call)'
Michael Pedhimey, Edq.and Amis Pan, Esq of Littler Mendelson, PC 415-677-4061 appering for Cross Defendnatrs Adams & Associates;

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT ADAMS & ASSOCIATES, INC.'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings

GRANTED without leave to amend as to the indemnity and declaratory relief causes of action. Plaintiff Reisner alleges employment claims that resulted in his termination. Those claims are a creature of an employment relationship. There are no allegations in the cross-complaint that Adams had an employment relationship with Plaintiff Reisner. Because no such relationship is alleged, Adams cannot be a joint tortfeasor and UBC cannot plead a claim for equitable indemnity.

"Where multiple tortfeasors are responsible for an indivisible injury suffered by the plaintiff, each tortfeasor is jointly and severally liable to the plaintiff for those damages and thus may be held individually liable to the injured plaintiff for the entirety of such damages." (Expressions at Rancho Niguel Ass'n v. Ahmanson Developments, Inc. (2001) 86 Cal.App.4th 1135, 1139.) As a matter of law, Adams cannot be considered a joint tortfeasor because it was not Reisner's employer.

"As a matter of law, only an employer can be liable for the tort of wrongful discharge in violation of public policy." (Khajavi v. Feather River Anesthesia Medical Group (2000) 84 Cal.App.4th 32, 53; accord, Miklosy v. Regents of University of California (2008) 44 Cal.4th 876, 898-901 [common law cause of action for wrongful termination in violation of public policy lies only against an employer].) Likewise, "the employer is liable for retaliation under section 12940, subdivision (h), but nonemployer individuals are not personally liable for their role in that retaliation." (Jones v. Lodge at Torrey Pines Partnership (2008) 42 Cal.4th 1158, 1173; see also Weinbaum v. Goldfarb, Whitman & Cohen (1996) 46 Cal.App.4th 1310, 1315 [nonemployer could not be liable for conspiracy to wrongfully discharge employee].)

order is signed.

AMENDED DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-07-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Richard Freeman Jr., Esq. 707-757-8206 for Plaintiff Jack Reisner.
Judy Juang, Esq. 213-488-4100 of Decarlo & Shanley for Defendant UNITED BROTHERHOOD OF CARPENTERS, DOUG MCCLAFFERTY, UNITED BROTHERHOOD OF CARPENTERS NATIONAL JOB CORP through Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff apologizes to the Court, states his dilemma, argues estoppel, Defendant argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument modifies the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT UNITED BROTHERHOOD OF CARPENTERS, DOUG MCCLAFFERTY, UNITED BROTHERHOOD OF CARPENTERS NATIONAL JOB CORP'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendants UBC, Doug McClafferty and Job Corps' demurrer to all three causes of action in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Jack Reisner is: a) is overruled as to the first cause of action for common law discrimination and retaliation against public policy against UBC; b) sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the first cause of action against Job Corps; c) overruled as to the second cause of action for violation of Labor Code 1102.5; d) sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the third case of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress against UBC and Mr. McClaferty; and e) sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the third cause of action against Job Corps. Liberally construed, the first cause of action alleges sufficient facts to support a wrongful termination claim against UBC based on Mr. Reisner's repeated requests for reasonable disability accommodation and his complaint about the treatment of minor students to an outside authority, but is untimely against JOB Corps. Mr. Reisner has sufficiently pled that UBC was his employer. The second cause of action is timely because it does not seek civil penalties (Ayala v. Frito Lay, Inc. (E.D. Cal. 2017) 263 F.Supp.3d 891, 917) and, liberally construed, alleges sufficient facts to support a violation of section1102.5 because the reasonableness of Mr. Reisner's belief in the illegality of defendants' actions is not amenable to resolution on a demurrer. If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Resnick is given leave to allege facts showing harassment on the basis of disability which can support an IIED cause of action. An IIED claim is time-barred as to Job Corps.

In addition, Mr. Reisner in good faith is given 20 days leave to amend to allege estoppel.

Plaintiff's Counsel to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-22-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT DOUG MCCLAFFERTY'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to all seven causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Jack Reisner is SUSTAINED without leave to amend to the first through third causes of action for violation of FEHA and the seventh cause of action for breach of the covenant of good faith and fair dealing and SUSTAINED with twenty days leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action harassment and retaliation in violation of public policy, the fifth cause of action for violation of Labor Code 1102.5 and the sixth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. Mr. Reisner is given leave to amend, if he can do so in good faith, to allege cognizable claims for wrongful termination due to retaliation in violation of public policy against the entity defendants only, wrongful termination due to disability and or religious discrimination in violation of public policy against the entity defendants only, violation Labor Code 1102.5 against the entity defendants only, and intentional infliction of emotional distress based on disability and/or religious harassment against all defendants. Mr. Reisner acknowledges that he is unable to allege a viable claim for violation of FEHA. California law does not recognize a claim for breach of the covenant of good faith and fair dealing based on discrimination, retaliation or harassment. Mr. Reisner's Tameny claim (his fourth action) does not sufficiently allege ultimate facts showing either discrimination or retaliation and there is no Tameny claim based on harassment. Mr. Reisner's section 1102.5 claim does not sufficiently allege protected activity covered by that code section. Mr. Reinser's IIED claim, while not barred by the workers' compensation exclusivity rule if based on harassment due to a protected characteristic, does not sufficiently allege ultimate facts showing extreme and outrageous conduct.
